figures.caption: Figure 5: Bifurcation study of the 1-D system (47) by using TPAPS and MCS. The bifurcation parameters are (a) the combination weight λ in the initial distribution, (b) the system parameter a, and (c) the noise amplitude σ. The resolution of each figure is 100× 100.

Figure 9: Efficient study of transient and near-stationary (t = 20) dynamics of the 2- D subcritcal system (50) using the TPAPS. (a) shows an example of marginal transient distributions of x at different times. (b) shows the Hopf bifurcation of stationary distributions triggered by the system parameter µ by visualizing the marginal stationary distributions of x. (c) investigates two-parameter bifurcation of stationary distributions depending on noise intensity D and system parameter µ in terms of the SD σx of the marginal distribution of x and (d) demonstrates the two-parameter bifurcation of the non-ergodic distributions depending on µ and the x-coordinate x0 of the center of initial distributions. (e) compares several groups of the SD σx of the distributions obtained by TPAPS and the MCS.
Figure 1: The architecture of the TPAPS q(x, t;Θ, pI) for jointly solving parameterized FPEs with diverse initial distributions and control parameters.
Figure 8: The training losses and test errors of the TPAPS on the 2-D subcritical system (50).

Table 3: The mean, SD, and median (MEAN/SD/MED) of the L1 errors between TPAPS and MCS results across 2,500 random transient distributions of the 2-D subcritical system (50) at various training stages.
Table 4: The mean, SD, and median (MEAN/SD/MED) of the L1 errors between TPAPS and MCS results across 2,500 random transient distributions of the Duffing system (51) at various training stages.
Figure 11: The training losses and test errors of the TPAPS on the 2-D Duffing system (51).
Figure 10: Four transient solutions of the 2-D Duffing system (51) by TPAPS and MCS with different initial distributions and system parameters.

Figure 12: The TPAPS results of the Duffing system (51) at early training stages. The initial distribution, system parameters, and the results at 106 training batches have been detailed in Fig. 10(d).
Figure 4: The training losses and test errors of the TPAPS on the 1-D system. The boxplots in (b) are summarized on 2,500 transient solutions with random initial distributions and system parameters.
Figure 3: The transient solutions of the 1-D system (47) by TPAPS and MCS with different initial distributions and system parameters.

Figure 7: Visualization of transient solutions of the 2-D subcritical system (50) with t ∈ [0, 20] in the 1st, 2nd, and 3rd PCs of the 50-D embedding space. The 9 transient solutions initialize from the distributions pIA, pIB, and pID in Fig. 6 with system parameters ΘA, ΘB, and ΘD and converge to three stationary distributions pSA, pSB, and pSD, which are attaching to the respective system parameters. For better illustration, similar to Fig. 2, the points in the background are embedding features of random Gaussian distributions N ([µ1, µ2] ⊤, diag{σ2 1, σ 2 2}), and the two lines of distributions G1 and G2 are defined in Eq. (45). The grey dashed lines indicate the axes of the three leading PCs.

Figure 13: Efficient study of transient and near-stationary (t = 20) dynamics of the Duffing system (51) using the TPAPS. (a) shows marginal transient distributions of x at different times. (b) shows the impact of the noise amplitude σ on the marginal stationary distribution of x at t = 20. (c) investigates the dynamics of stationary distributions depending on the damping parameters η and the stiffness parameter β. The true transient solutions are obtained by MCS in (a2) and the true stationary results in (b2) and (c2) are calculated by Eq. (52). The resolution of all subfigures is 200× 100.
Figure 6: Four transient solutions of the 2-D subcritical system (50) by TPAPS and MCS with different initial distributions and system parameters.

Figure 2: Visualization of the GMD autoencoder. (a) Six GMDs and their reconstructions. (b) The PCA and distance visualizations of the 50-D embedding space. Each point represents a 2-D Gaussian distribution in Eq. (43). The three lines consist of continuously changing distributions, where µ1 = [−2,−2]⊤, µ2 = [2, 2]⊤, and I is the 2-D identity matrix.
